scope: Counterfactual seven-step provider-leverage mechanism at pinned upstream commit 5ceb305b30b4c82653c9b6642499c12e946ec319
Strongest benign architecture using the same visible code
Comparative explanatory-power assessment bounded to static repository evidence

findings.title: Step 1 — useful legal tooling is designed for deployment with connector access

evidence.description: The plugin declares remote Ironclad, DocuSign, iManage, Slack, and Google Drive connectors capable of searching or reading customer repositories, subject to runtime permissions.
The deployment helper is designed to upload skills and create managed agents through provider API endpoints.

source_to_sink: customer legal repository or workflow
declared remote MCP connector or managed-agent deployment
legal plugin or hosted agent analysis
runtime destination and actual grants remain unobserved
findings.proves: The repository is technically designed to support connector-mediated legal work and hosted-agent deployment, making step 1 coherent as an architecture.
findings.does_not_prove: It does not prove that any customer deployed these components, granted a particular permission, transmitted data, or adopted the product because of its value.
findings.alternative_explanation: The connectors and deployment tooling exist solely to perform customer-requested work under customer-selected permissions.
findings.test_to_confirm: Obtain a tenant configuration export, connector authorization grants, deployment receipts, and runtime request logs showing the exact data paths and operators.

findings.title: Step 2 — onboarding explicitly captures firm-specific playbooks and operating rules

evidence.description: The interview requests signed agreements and an escalation matrix, then extracts actual positions and deltas from stated positions.
The workflow asks where executed contracts live, requests templates and signed agreements, and computes the delta described as the real playbook.
The practice-profile template encodes side-dependent risk appetite, fallback terms, approval thresholds, liability, indemnity, IP, and termination positions.

source_to_sink: attorney answers, escalation matrix, templates, and signed agreements
cold-start extraction and delta computation
customer-specific plain-text practice profile
later skills read the profile
findings.proves: Static instructions deliberately elicit and encode a customer-specific operating playbook, including risk appetite, exceptions, escalation rules, and local terminology.
findings.does_not_prove: It does not prove provider custody, uniqueness, protectability, retention, training, or cross-customer use of the resulting profile.
findings.alternative_explanation: Customer-specific configuration is necessary to provide accurate, requested legal assistance and is kept as customer-editable local configuration.
findings.test_to_confirm: For deployed behavior, capture the exact profile write location, file ownership and access controls, inference payloads, connector reads, and tenant-level retention configuration.

findings.title: Step 3 — routine use is designed to record deviations, rationales, outcomes, and accept/reject decisions

evidence.description: The weekly debrief captures why deviations were accepted and feeds a local deviation log read by the playbook monitor.
The debrief schema records playbook versus signed position, severity, a categorized basis, optional context, and one-off exclusions.
The monitor groups consistent deviations and their bases, then drafts a proposed playbook revision with supporting data.
An attorney accepts, rejects, edits, or defers each proposal; writes require an exact diff and explicit confirmation.

source_to_sink: signed deviation and attorney rationale
customer-local deviation-log.yaml
pattern grouping and proposed profile update
attorney accept, reject, edit, or defer decision
confirmed customer-local profile write
findings.proves: The static design creates a high-value customer-specific calibration loop that can encode context, choice, rationale, and outcome relationships.
findings.does_not_prove: It does not prove automatic model learning, remote retention, provider derivation, or any use outside the customer's requested workflow.
findings.alternative_explanation: The loop is a transparent, local knowledge-management feature with one-off exclusions and human approval, intended to keep customer instructions accurate.
findings.test_to_confirm: Observe a synthetic deployed debrief end to end and compare local writes, inference payloads, telemetry, hosted logs, deletion behavior, and support access.

findings.title: Step 4 — provider retention or derivation of structured customer signals is not established

evidence.description: Visible instructions place the populated practice profile at a version-independent user configuration path and describe this repository file as a replaceable template.
The visible persistence destination for debrief knowledge is a user-path deviation log; the source says nothing about inference-service or telemetry retention.

source_to_sink: customer-local profile or deviation log
model inference or hosted feature may process content
provider retention or structured derivation is unobserved
secondary-use destination is unobserved
findings.proves: The repository identifies customer-local persistence but leaves provider-side inference, telemetry, support, and hosted-agent retention outside source scope.
findings.does_not_prove: Local persistence does not prove zero provider processing or retention; conversely, model processing capability does not prove durable provider retention or derivation.
findings.alternative_explanation: The provider processes only what is necessary to answer the customer's request under contractual retention controls, while durable operational memory remains customer-controlled.
findings.test_to_confirm: Obtain feature-specific data-flow documentation, raw synthetic request and response captures, telemetry schemas, retention and deletion logs, support-access audit logs, subprocessors, and controlling contract terms.

findings.title: Step 5 — cross-organization normalization into a common ontology is not established

evidence.description: The observed normalization is within one customer's templates, signed agreements, agreement types, and counterparty sizes, written into that customer's profile.
Pattern grouping is specified over one local deviation log and stops locally when no threshold is crossed.

source_to_sink: customer-specific profile and deviation signals
within-customer normalization is visible
cross-customer aggregation mechanism is absent from source
common provider ontology and operator are unobserved
findings.proves: The code supports within-customer normalization, not cross-organization pooling.
findings.does_not_prove: The absence of cross-customer code in this repository does not establish that no separate provider system performs aggregation, but it supplies no affirmative evidence that one does.
findings.alternative_explanation: All normalization is tenant-specific adaptation used only to improve that customer's playbook.
findings.test_to_confirm: Require authenticated architecture diagrams, tenant-isolation tests, aggregation job definitions, feature-store lineage, organization-key handling, access logs, and contractual restrictions on cross-customer derivation.

findings.title: Step 6 — generalized workflow, evaluation, routing, or vertical-product improvement is not established

evidence.description: The only evidenced improvement target is a specific customer's playbook language, supported by that customer's deviation entries.
The visible update is customer-approved and writes to the customer-specific profile; no generalized evaluation or product pipeline is identified.

source_to_sink: customer-specific calibrated signal
customer-local proposal and approval are visible
generalized training, evaluation, routing, or product-improvement pipeline is absent from source
generalized artifact and consumers are unobserved
findings.proves: The repository demonstrates customer-specific improvement but contains no reproducible source-to-sink path to generalized provider improvement.
findings.does_not_prove: It does not establish that generalized improvement is impossible or that all provider systems are represented in this repository.
findings.alternative_explanation: The calibration loop improves only the customer's local configuration, while the base service remains governed by separate commercial data-use controls.
findings.test_to_confirm: Inspect training and evaluation dataset lineage, feedback-routing rules, feature flags, model or workflow release notes tied to customer-derived signals, opt-in records, and independent deletion tests.

findings.title: Step 7 — architectural dependency is plausible, while adjacent-market displacement is unestablished

evidence.description: Every plugin skill is instructed to read a shared version-independent profile, making that configuration operationally central while keeping it in a plain user path.
The product explicitly describes a month-of-use calibration effect and proposes policy updates from repeated deviations.
Skills and callable agents can be uploaded and referenced through hosted provider agent APIs, creating a plausible service dependency when that mode is chosen.

source_to_sink: accumulated customer configuration and calibration
many plugin skills depend on the profile
optional hosted skill and agent deployment
switching effort is plausible
bundling, intermediary weakening, and market effects are unobserved
findings.proves: Accumulated configuration and hosted deployment can plausibly create switching effort or bargaining leverage even without covert secondary use.
findings.does_not_prove: It does not prove lock-in is material, data is non-portable, adjacent legal work is bundled, firms are weakened, or the provider intends disintermediation.
findings.alternative_explanation: Plain-text local profiles, direct editability, and customer-controlled connectors reduce switching costs and are ordinary product-integration choices.
findings.test_to_confirm: Measure complete export and re-import fidelity, dependency on provider-only identifiers or APIs, termination and deletion behavior, migration time, contractual exit rights, and comparative performance after migration.

findings.title: The benign customer-adaptation architecture currently explains the visible code better than covert secondary use

evidence.description: Durable practice configuration is described as customer-local, shared across plugins, editable, and separate from replaceable plugin templates.
Policy changes require attorney choice, an exact diff, and explicit confirmation; reject and defer do not modify the profile.
The document-reader leaf is declared read-only, treats document bytes as data, limits outbound access to configured VDR MCPs, and enables only specified connectors.
The orchestrator is limited to local read tools, with MCP and web access delegated to bounded leaves and only one writer leaf.

source_to_sink: customer supplies firm-specific material for requested work
bounded skills and connectors process it
customer-local profiles and logs preserve reusable memory
human gates control policy mutation
no visible cross-customer or generalized-reuse sink
findings.proves: H0 explains every confirmed static component without requiring the missing retention, pooling, and generalized-reuse mechanisms. H1 remains plausible through dependency and product insight. H2 has lower explanatory support because its decisive steps 4 through 6 are unknown, not observed.
findings.does_not_prove: This comparison does not validate runtime enforcement, contract compliance, zero provider retention, or the impossibility of undisclosed systems outside the repository.
findings.alternative_explanation: A hostile architecture could reside entirely outside the public repository, but that possibility has no greater evidentiary weight without runtime, contractual, or source-to-sink evidence.
findings.test_to_confirm: Run synthetic canary tests across inference, connectors, feedback, support, telemetry, and hosted agents; reconcile observations with tenant-specific contracts, deletion proofs, and independent technical audit evidence.

new_patterns.name: Provider-retained structured derivative
new_patterns.logic: Detect a complete path from customer-specific legal signal through provider-controlled durable storage to a structured derivative, with retention authority and tenant boundary identified.
new_patterns.false_positive_risk: Local customer persistence, transient inference processing, or aggregate service metrics can be mistaken for provider-retained customer ontology.

new_patterns.name: Cross-organization normalization
new_patterns.logic: Require evidence that customer-originated signals cross tenant boundaries into a common taxonomy, benchmark, router, workflow, or product artifact.
new_patterns.false_positive_risk: Common static schemas and generic legal taxonomies do not establish pooling of customer-derived signals.

new_patterns.name: Portable-exit fidelity
new_patterns.logic: Measure whether accumulated profile, rationale, proposal, and outcome genealogy can be exported and reproduced outside provider-specific APIs without material loss.
new_patterns.false_positive_risk: Use of a hosted API or ordinary integration effort is not by itself material lock-in.

negative_evidence.description: The populated practice profile is designated for a user-controlled, version-independent plain-text configuration path; the repository copy is only a replaceable template.
negative_evidence.effect: Supports H0 and constrains any claim that this repository visibly stores the durable firm profile in provider-controlled shared infrastructure.

negative_evidence.description: A proposed policy change is not written until an attorney reviews an exact diff and explicitly confirms; rejected and deferred proposals do not alter the profile.
negative_evidence.effect: Contradicts an automatic-learning characterization of the visible playbook update loop and supports a customer-governed adaptation explanation.

negative_evidence.description: The untrusted-document reader is declared read-only, schema-bound, and limited to configured VDR connectors, with unused connectors disabled.
negative_evidence.effect: Provides affirmative design counterevidence to unrestricted tool egress, while leaving deployed enforcement unknown.

negative_evidence.description: The orchestrator is scoped to local read tools; network access is isolated to leaves and only a designated leaf has write authority.
negative_evidence.effect: Supports least-authority and separation-of-duties as a benign architectural explanation.

unknowns.question: What exact customer content, derivatives, and metadata leave local custody during inference, connector use, feedback, support, telemetry, and hosted-agent operation?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: The public repository contains static prompts, manifests, and deployment helpers, not the proprietary runtime, tenant grants, network captures, or production logs.
unknowns.needed_evidence: Synthetic request and network captures, connector audit logs, telemetry schemas and samples, hosted-agent traces, data-flow diagrams, and tenant configuration exports.

unknowns.question: What is retained, for how long, under whose control, and with what deletion and support-access guarantees?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: Visible local paths describe customer persistence but do not specify provider-side feature retention or contractual overrides.
unknowns.needed_evidence: Feature-specific contract terms, privacy and retention schedules, subprocessors, support-access logs, deletion receipts, backups policy, and independent audit results.

unknowns.question: Are any firm-specific signals normalized across organizations or used in generalized training, evaluation, routing, workflow, or vertical-product development?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: No cross-customer aggregation, dataset lineage, or generalized-improvement sink is present in the reviewed source.
unknowns.needed_evidence: Dataset and feature-store lineage, aggregation jobs, tenant-key controls, training and evaluation manifests, feedback-routing records, opt-in records, and release genealogy.

unknowns.question: How portable is the accumulated firm profile and its decision genealogy at exit?
unknowns.why_source_cannot_answer: Plain-text profile storage favors portability, but hosted skill IDs, agent IDs, connector state, logs, and full migration fidelity are not tested here.
unknowns.needed_evidence: A reproducible export, deletion, re-import, and comparative-function test covering profiles, deviation logs, proposals, rationales, outcomes, connector state, and hosted agents.
